title: Curry/Turmeric on Cotton
stainChemistry: Curry stains are dominated by curcumin from turmeric — one of the most persistent natural dyes. On cotton, curcumin bonds through strong hydrogen bonds and is intensified by alkaline conditions and UV light. The oil base (ghee, coconut oil) drives the pigment deep into fibers. Secondary spices add additional pigments and oils.
whyThisWorks: Avoiding sunlight and alkali is the first priority — both permanently intensify curcumin color on cotton. Oil is addressed first with POG or surfactant. An oxidizer (H2O2) then breaks the conjugated double bonds in curcumin that produce the yellow color. This is one of the hardest stains to fully remove — professional treatment may be necessary for complete resolution.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Remove excess solids with dull edge — do not rub
2. Apply Tannin Formula for the plant-pigment component
3. Apply POG for the oil component
4. Flush with NSD and cool water
5. If color persists: apply H2O2 6% on colorfast fabrics
6. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse. Blot dry

homeSolutions.agent: Know the rules: sunlight is your friend, alkaline is your enemy
homeSolutions.instruction: Turmeric (curcumin) is the hardest common household stain. Two counter-intuitive rules: (1) ALKALINE products turn a yellow stain BRIGHT RED and lock it in forever — ammonia, baking soda, many all-purpose sprays. Avoid them. (2) SUNLIGHT, by contrast, breaks down curcumin — UV is literally the best home finish for this stain. Most other stains hide from the sun; this one you want to expose to it.

On an inside seam, dab a drop of Dawn + 3% peroxide and blot after 60 seconds. If the fabric holds color, you're safe. Meanwhile, lift off any food chunks or oil pooling with a butter knife or credit card edge — away from the center. Don't rub — mashing curry chunks into the weave makes every following step harder.

Turn the fabric inside out and run cold water through the BACK of the stain for 30–60 seconds. Push the curry sauce and its oil base OUT the way it came in. Skip hot water — heat polymerizes the oil base (ghee, coconut oil) into the cotton and makes the pigment even harder to reach.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap — cut the oil carrier first
homeSolutions.instruction: Put a small drop of clear Dawn directly on the stain (no water yet). Work it in with a fingertip for 30 seconds. Let sit 10 minutes. The oil or ghee base of curry traps the curcumin pigment against the fiber — Dawn emulsifies the oil so the pigment-fighting steps can actually reach the pigment. Blot with a damp cloth.

Mix one tablespoon of Dawn with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide. Dab onto the stain with a white cloth, let sit 10–15 minutes, then blot. Curcumin fights back — plan on 3–5 cycles for a fresh stain, more for dried. Between cycles, re-dab the solution rather than flooding. Peroxide breaks the conjugated double bonds in curcumin that produce the yellow color.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach. On brightly colored cotton, the step-2 patch test is your safety net.

homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on cold with your normal detergent. Hang to air-dry, still damp. DO NOT put it in the dryer yet — heat on residual curcumin sets the color permanently and also turns any alkaline laundry-booster residue into a bright red stain you can't undo.

This step is what actually finishes the job. Lay the still-damp garment flat outdoors in direct sunlight for 2–4 hours. UV breaks down curcumin molecules much faster than any home chemistry. You will often watch a bright yellow shadow fade to nothing over a single afternoon. Keep the fabric damp — re-spray with cold water if it dries out (damp + sunlight is more effective than dry + sunlight). On colored cotton, sunlight may fade your dye too — keep exposure under 2 hours and watch closely.

homeSolutions.agent: Second round if any shadow remains
homeSolutions.instruction: After sunlight: inspect in daylight. If a faint yellow trace persists, repeat steps 5–7 once more. Two full rounds is the home ceiling for curcumin — beyond that, the pigment has cross-linked with fiber and you need professional chemistry.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a dry cleaner for stubborn turmeric
homeSolutions.instruction: If two rounds of home + sunlight don't clear it, escalate. Tell them: "Curry (turmeric) on cotton, age [fresh/dried/heat-set]. Treated at home with Dawn + 3% peroxide x 2 rounds + sunlight finish. No alkaline products used." That's the exact protocol pros would follow at home-grade potency — they can step up to 6% peroxide, commercial tannin formulas, and reducing agents. Don't expect 100% removal on old or heat-set curry — curcumin is famously the final boss of household stains.

steps: 1. Remove solids with a spoon or dull edge — scrape, don't rub
2. Rinse with cold water to remove loose material
3. Apply Dawn dish soap. Work gently
4. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat if needed
5. Air dry — no heat until stain is gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 rounds of treatment. Combo stains are inherently harder — a professional cleaner has specialized chemistry for each component
warnings: Treat each component in order — don't skip steps
Cold water always — heat sets protein components permanently

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If curry stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is curry on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Can you identify the specific components?
Is there a greasy/oily feel to the stain?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
